source_text: The house is red.

reference_translations.text: La casa è rossa.
reference_translations.notes: Standard rendering.

explanation: Casa is feminine singular. Rosso is a Class I adjective with the four-form pattern; f.sg takes -a, giving rossa. So la casa è rossa.

source_text: I have two black cats.

reference_translations.text: Ho due gatti neri.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: Ho due gatti neri a casa.
reference_translations.notes: Optional adverbial; same agreement test.

reference_translations.text: Ho due gatti nero.
reference_translations.notes: Classic no-agreement miss.

gender.reveal: gatto is masculine (m); gatta is the feminine

explanation: Gatti is m.pl. Nero is Class I, so the m.pl form takes -i: neri. Colour adjectives sit in post-noun position by default: gatti neri.

source_text: The girls are very intelligent.

reference_translations.text: Le ragazze sono molto intelligenti.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: Le ragazze sono molto intelligente.
reference_translations.notes: Classic miss for the tree-noted hardest case: no number agreement on Class II adjective.

explanation: The tree-noted hardest case for English learners: Class II f.pl takes -i, not -e. Ragazze is f.pl, intelligente is Class II, so the form is intelligenti. The classic miss leaves intelligente unchanged.

source_text: The walls of the kitchen are white.

reference_translations.text: I muri della cucina sono bianchi.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: Le pareti della cucina sono bianche.
reference_translations.notes: Pareti (f.pl) is also a valid translation of 'walls' (interior walls particularly).

reference_translations.text: I muri della cucina sono bianci.
reference_translations.notes: Soft-c miss: forgets to insert h.

explanation: Muri is m.pl. Bianco is a penult-stressed -co adjective: stem change inserts h to preserve the hard c-sound, giving bianchi. Pareti (the alternative for 'walls') is f.pl and would take bianche by the parallel rule.

source_text: My colleagues are very nice.

reference_translations.text: I miei colleghi sono molto simpatici.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: I miei colleghi sono molto simpatichi.
reference_translations.notes: Over-application of -chi rule (forgets antepenult-stress exception).

translation.reveal: colleague - collega (same form m and f)

gender.reveal: common-gender noun; m.pl colleghi, f.pl colleghe

explanation: Simpatico has antepenult stress (sim-pa-ti-co), so the stress rule gives m.pl simpatici with soft c, not simpatichi. Compare with bianchi: there, penult stress preserves the hard c through h-insertion.

source_text: Marco and Luigi have been friends for many years.

reference_translations.text: Marco e Luigi sono amici da molti anni.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; note Italian uses present + 'da' for ongoing duration.

reference_translations.text: Marco e Luigi sono amichi da molti anni.
reference_translations.notes: Over-application of the predictable -chi rule.

explanation: Amico is irregular in the m.pl: the actual form is amici with soft c, despite penultimate stress that would predict amichi. This is a learned exception to the regular h-insertion rule.

source_text: The boys and girls are Italian.

reference_translations.text: I ragazzi e le ragazze sono italiani.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Mixed-gender plural defaults to masculine.

reference_translations.text: I ragazzi e le ragazze sono italiane.
reference_translations.notes: Proximity-agreement miss (matches the closest noun).

explanation: Italian defaults to the masculine plural form for mixed-gender groups, even when the closest noun is feminine. So i ragazzi e le ragazze sono italiani, not italiane.

source_text: She has pink shoes.

reference_translations.text: Ha delle scarpe rosa.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Rosa is invariable.

reference_translations.text: Lei ha delle scarpe rosa.
reference_translations.notes: Subject pronoun optional but possible for emphasis.

reference_translations.text: Ha delle scarpe rose.
reference_translations.notes: Over-applies Class I plural to an invariable colour adjective. Note 'rose' is also the noun 'roses'.

explanation: Rosa is an invariable adjective borrowed from the noun 'rose' (the flower). It doesn't inflect for gender or number, so scarpe rosa stays unchanged in the plural.

source_text: I want a green car.

reference_translations.text: Voglio una macchina verde.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Colour adjective post-noun.

reference_translations.text: Vorrei una macchina verde.
reference_translations.notes: Conditional 'vorrei' is more polite; same agreement test.

reference_translations.text: Voglio una verde macchina.
reference_translations.notes: English-style fronting of the colour adjective.

explanation: Macchina is f.sg. Verde is a Class II adjective: same form for both genders in the singular, so still verde. Colour adjectives sit in post-noun position by default: una macchina verde.

source_text: He is a long-standing friend of mine.

reference_translations.text: È un mio vecchio amico.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Vecchio pre-noun = long-standing (relational reading).

reference_translations.text: È un vecchio amico.
reference_translations.notes: Without 'mio', still good; the pre-noun position carries the same meaning.

reference_translations.text: È un mio amico vecchio.
reference_translations.notes: Wrong reading: post-noun vecchio means 'elderly friend', not 'long-standing'.

explanation: Vecchio shifts meaning by position. Pre-noun is the relational reading: long-standing. Un mio vecchio amico = a friend of many years. Post-noun (un amico vecchio) would mean physically elderly.
examiner_note: Tests both the canonical pre-noun position and the semantic shift. The negative reference illustrates the reading the learner must avoid.

source_text: Leonardo was a great man.

reference_translations.text: Leonardo è stato un grande uomo.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Pre-noun grande = great (figurative).

reference_translations.text: Leonardo fu un grande uomo.
reference_translations.notes: Passato remoto register, common in historical/narrative context.

reference_translations.text: Leonardo è stato un uomo grande.
reference_translations.notes: Wrong reading: post-noun grande means 'physically big/tall'.

gender.reveal: uomo is masculine (m); irregular plural uomini

explanation: Grande shifts meaning by position. Pre-noun is the figurative reading: great in stature, achievement. Un grande uomo = a great man, like Leonardo. Post-noun (un uomo grande) would mean physically big. The shortened gran uomo also exists but is rarer.

source_text: What a beautiful book!

reference_translations.text: Che bel libro!
reference_translations.notes: Standard exclamative. Bel before m.sg consonant.

reference_translations.text: Che bello libro!
reference_translations.notes: Wrong allomorph: bello stays full only before s+consonant/z, not before regular consonants.

explanation: Pre-noun bello has an allomorph series paralleling the definite article. Before m.sg nouns taking il (most consonants), bello becomes bel: che bel libro, parallel to il libro.

source_text: Have a good day!

reference_translations.text: Buona giornata!
reference_translations.notes: Standard greeting. Buona before f.sg consonant.

reference_translations.text: Buon pomeriggio!
reference_translations.notes: If 'day' is paraphrased as 'afternoon'; tests the m.sg buon form.

reference_translations.text: Buono giornata!
reference_translations.notes: Over-applies the full form; should be buona.

explanation: Pre-noun buono takes the f.sg form buona before a consonant: buona giornata, a standard greeting/farewell. The wrong buono giornata over-applies the m.sg form.

source_text: Those backpacks are very heavy.

reference_translations.text: Quegli zaini sono molto pesanti.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Quegli before m.pl vowel/s+cons/z.

reference_translations.text: Quei zaini sono molto pesanti.
reference_translations.notes: Wrong allomorph: quei is for m.pl consonant; before z-initial nouns, use quegli.

gender.reveal: zaino is masculine (m); plural takes gli

explanation: Pre-noun quello has an allomorph series like the definite article. Before m.pl nouns taking gli (vowel-initial, s+consonant, z), the form is quegli — parallel to gli zaini. Pesante is Class II: m.pl pesanti.

source_text: She was wearing a navy blue dress.

reference_translations.text: Indossava un vestito blu marino.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Compound colour blu marino is invariable.

reference_translations.text: Aveva un vestito blu marino.
reference_translations.notes: Avere as a possessive paraphrase of 'wearing'.

reference_translations.text: Indossava un vestito blu marini.
reference_translations.notes: Over-applied agreement on a compound colour.

explanation: Compound colours like blu marino are invariable as a unit: they don't inflect for the noun's gender or number. Un vestito blu marino stays unchanged.

source_text: Le mele rosse sono nel cestino.

reference_translations.text: The red apples are in the basket.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: The red apples are in the little basket.
reference_translations.notes: Cestino can also imply a smaller basket.

explanation: Mele is f.pl. Rosso is a Class I adjective; f.pl takes -e, giving rosse. So le mele rosse: 'the red apples'.

source_text: Una persona gentile è sempre apprezzata.

reference_translations.text: A kind person is always appreciated.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: Kind people are always appreciated.
reference_translations.notes: Generic English plural rendering of the singular Italian generic; acceptable.

gender.reveal: feminine (f); used for both males and females

explanation: Persona is f.sg. Gentile is a Class II adjective: same form for both genders in the singular. Class II is invariable for gender in the singular, so still gentile.

source_text: Carlo è un caro amico.

reference_translations.text: Carlo is a dear friend.
reference_translations.notes: Pre-noun caro = dear/beloved.

reference_translations.text: Carlo is a close friend.
reference_translations.notes: Pragmatically equivalent; close = dear in the affective sense.

reference_translations.text: Carlo is an expensive friend.
reference_translations.notes: Wrong reading: 'expensive' is the post-noun (literal/price) meaning.

explanation: Caro shifts meaning by position. Pre-noun is the figurative/affective reading: dear, beloved. Un caro amico = a dear friend. Post-noun (un amico caro) would mean financially expensive — concrete and almost humorous in context.

source_text: Mi piacciono i fiori lilla.

reference_translations.text: I like lilac flowers.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: I like the lilac flowers.
reference_translations.notes: Italian generic plural with definite article often translates to bare English plural; both fine.

explanation: Lilla, borrowed from the flower name, is an invariable adjective: it stays lilla in singular and plural alike. I fiori lilla = 'the lilac flowers'.

source_text: Sono dei begli zaini in vetrina.

reference_translations.text: There are some beautiful backpacks in the window.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'Begli' tests the m.pl allomorph before vowel/s+cons/z.

reference_translations.text: Those are nice backpacks in the display.
reference_translations.notes: Slightly different framing; same agreement content.

explanation: Begli is the m.pl form of pre-noun bello before nouns taking gli. Zaini begins with z, so the article would be gli zaini and the agreeing adjective is begli zaini.

source_text: I libri antichi sono in biblioteca.

reference_translations.text: The old books are in the library.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'Antichi' tests the counter-irregular -chi despite antepenult stress.

reference_translations.text: The ancient books are in the library.
reference_translations.notes: 'Ancient' is also a valid translation, often preferred for the figurative/historical sense.

explanation: Antico is a counter-irregular: despite antepenult stress (an-ti-co), the m.pl is antichi with h-insertion, not antici as the stress rule would predict. Compare simpatici, which does follow the stress rule.

source_text: Le strade di questa zona sono molto lunghe.

reference_translations.text: The streets in this area are very long.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: The roads in this neighbourhood are very long.
reference_translations.notes: Strada can be road or street; zona can be area, district, or neighbourhood.

explanation: Strade is f.pl. Lungo is a -go adjective: stem change inserts h to preserve the hard g-sound in the f.pl, giving lunghe.

source_text: Il povero ragazzo è caduto dalla bicicletta.

reference_translations.text: The poor boy fell off his bicycle.
reference_translations.notes: 'Poor' here = unfortunate, the pre-noun reading.

reference_translations.text: The unfortunate boy fell off his bicycle.
reference_translations.notes: Makes the pre-noun reading explicit.

reference_translations.text: The boy with little money fell off his bicycle.
reference_translations.notes: Wrong reading: that would be 'un ragazzo povero' (post-noun, literal financial meaning).

explanation: Povero shifts meaning by position. Pre-noun is the empathetic reading: unfortunate, to be pitied. Il povero ragazzo è caduto = the boy (poor thing) fell. Post-noun would mean financially poor.

source_text: Sono le sei e mezza.

reference_translations.text: It is half past six.
reference_translations.notes: Standard time expression.

reference_translations.text: It's six thirty.
reference_translations.notes: Informal English equivalent.

translation.reveal: mezzo - half (here: mezza agrees with implicit ora)

explanation: In Italian time expressions, mezza is the f.sg form of mezzo, agreeing with an implicit 'ora' (hour). Sono le sei e mezza = 'it's half past six'.

source_text: I miei colleghi e le mie colleghe sono stanchi dopo il viaggio.

reference_translations.text: My colleagues (male and female) are tired after the trip.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Italian masculine plural covers mixed-gender 'colleagues' in English.

reference_translations.text: My (male and female) colleagues are tired after the journey.
reference_translations.notes: Same meaning, different glossing of viaggio.

translation.reveal: collega - colleague (same form m and f)

explanation: Two rules combine: mixed-gender plural defaults to m.pl, and stanco's -co stem takes h-insertion in the m.pl. Both give stanchi.

source_text: La chiesa di sant'Antonio è qui vicino.

reference_translations.text: Saint Anthony's church is nearby.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: The church of Saint Anthony is near here.
reference_translations.notes: More literal rendering.

explanation: Pre-noun santo elides before a vowel-initial male saint's name: sant'Antonio. This is elision, parallel to the il → l' shift in the article system.

source_text: Marco ha gli occhi grigi e i capelli lisci.

reference_translations.text: Marco has grey eyes and straight hair.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Tests both -gio→-gi (grigi) and -cio→-ci (lisci).

reference_translations.text: Marco has grey eyes and smooth hair.
reference_translations.notes: Liscio = smooth or straight (of hair).

explanation: Both -gio and -cio adjectives drop the unstressed -i in the m.pl. Grigio → grigi (gio_gi rule), liscio → lisci (cio_ci rule). Same underlying stem change applied to two adjective patterns.

source_text: Quella casa nuova è di mio fratello.

reference_translations.text: That new house is my brother's.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'New' here is the literal post-noun reading: a recently-built or freshly-acquired house.

reference_translations.text: That new house belongs to my brother.
reference_translations.notes: Equivalent phrasing.

explanation: Quella is the f.sg form of pre-noun quello before a consonant, parallel to la casa. Nuovo in post-noun position gives the literal reading: recently built or acquired, not 'another'.

source_text: Il professore è un mio vecchio amico.

reference_translations.text: The professor is a long-standing friend of mine.
reference_translations.notes: Captures the pre-noun reading explicitly.

reference_translations.text: The professor is an old friend of mine.
reference_translations.notes: Idiomatic English: 'old friend' defaults to the long-standing reading.

reference_translations.text: The professor is an elderly friend of mine.
reference_translations.notes: Wrong reading: would correspond to 'un amico vecchio' (post-noun).

explanation: Vecchio pre-noun is the relational reading: long-standing. Un mio vecchio amico = a friend of many years, not a friend who is elderly. The English 'old friend' carries the same default idiomatically.

source_text: Garibaldi è stato un grande condottiero.

reference_translations.text: Garibaldi was a great commander.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Pre-noun grande = great (figurative).

reference_translations.text: Garibaldi was a great military leader.
reference_translations.notes: Condottiero glossed more broadly.

reference_translations.text: Garibaldi was a big commander.
reference_translations.notes: Wrong reading: literal/physical, would require 'un condottiero grande'.

explanation: Grande pre-noun gives the figurative reading: great in stature, reputation, achievement. Garibaldi un grande condottiero — recognised for his leadership, not his physical size. Post-noun grande would be the literal physical reading.

source_text: La povera signora ha perso le chiavi della macchina.

reference_translations.text: The poor lady has lost her car keys.
reference_translations.notes: English 'poor lady' in this context naturally reads as the empathetic/unfortunate sense.

reference_translations.text: The unfortunate lady has lost her car keys.
reference_translations.notes: Makes the pre-noun reading explicit.

reference_translations.text: The lady with little money has lost her car keys.
reference_translations.notes: Wrong reading: post-noun (impoverished) would be 'una signora povera'.

explanation: Povero pre-noun is the empathetic reading: unfortunate, to be pitied. La povera signora ha perso le chiavi — sympathy with her predicament. Post-noun (una signora povera) would mean financially poor.
